article_meta.title: Supreme Court ruling against Trump’s tariffs is unlikely to mean an end to trade policy chaos 1 of 3 | Containers are st

reasoning: Mostly neutral paraphrase with some minor inheritance of the article’s loaded framing (e.g., “chaos,” “administrative headaches”). It avoids the strongest rhetoric from the source and adds some attribution/context, but a few interpretive phrases remain in the model’s own voice.

custom_reasoning.spin: Uses a few interpretive terms like “chaos,” “confusion,” and “contentious,” but generally sticks to factual descriptions.
custom_reasoning.unsubstantiated_claims: Claims are grounded in the article’s reporting (legal limits, Section 122/301, EU delay, refund uncertainty).
custom_reasoning.opinion_as_fact: “Chaos” and “administrative headaches” are presented in the model’s voice rather than attributed.
custom_reasoning.sensationalism: Tone is measured overall; “chaos” introduces mild sensationalism but less than the source’s “stunning rebuke/chaotic/mess.”
custom_reasoning.mind_reading: No assertions about motives or intentions.
custom_reasoning.slant: Balanced treatment of legal tools and international reactions; slight emphasis on uncertainty.
custom_reasoning.bias_by_omission: Omits some countervailing points present in the article (e.g., analysts expecting partners to stick with deals, Treasury’s claim on tariff revenues).
custom_reasoning.word_choice: Generally neutral; avoids politically charged labels; “so-called unfair” adds distancing.
custom_reasoning.negativity_bias: Highlights uncertainty and administrative burdens more than stabilizing context.
custom_reasoning.elite_populist_bias: No systematic preference for elite vs. grassroots sources.
custom_reasoning.framing_neutrality: More neutral than the source but inherits a bit of its framing (“chaos”).
custom_reasoning.perspective_completeness: Captures multiple perspectives (experts, EU, officials) but misses some (Treasury stance, analysts expecting deal continuity).
